widgets_values:
The "Identifier" fields are for detecting the character you want to detail. Fields 1, 2, 3, and 6 are hooked up to SAM3, so use natural language to describe what you want. For subject detection, I find that some combination of: age, hair color, worn clothing, skin color, and positioning in the photo usually gets me what I want. When generating multi-character images, specify their positions from left to right so you don't have to redo this. 

For face, always specify "the face and hair of". 

The Identifier fields are in the same order as the labelled green dots on the right side of the node.

Fields four and five use YOLO detection because SAM3 doesn't recognize NSFW concepts very well. 

For breast detection, you can put in "all" to detail all instances simultaneously, or you can use "1", "2" and so on to specify a single instance going from left to right. 

You can get the breast detection YOLO model here: https://huggingface.co/datasets/dubsta/xmas/blob/main/female-breast-v4.7.pt
Obviously, you'll need SAM3 or 3.1. You can get it without waiting for the gating request from the mirror here: https://huggingface.co/1038lab/sam3

The "Genital Detector" node allows you to easily change the YOLO model used to detect the genitals of that character. 

You can use these YOLO models for detection of penises and vaginas: https://huggingface.co/art0123/Models_collection/tree/main/bbox

Ironically, you can also use this anti-NSFW YOLO model to detect sexual penetration: https://huggingface.co/erax-ai/EraX-Anti-NSFW-V1.1

For the first set of YOLO models, they support indexing just like breast detection. 

For the anti-NSFW YOLO model, it accepts: anus, make_love, nipple, penis, vagina.
Each refinement step is previewed here, while the upscaler preview is over in the Main Control Group. All comparisons are with the original image.

The main control group contains easy access to most of the basic controls, allowing you to quickly change parameters across all 24 detailer nodes with one change here. 

The "Refiner" node contains the settings for the Detailing portion of the workflow. Increasing the steps tends to cause severe artifacts. If you get poor results, try using the "BigLove" ZIT fine tune. 

The "Upscaler" node contains similar controls for the upscaler step. 

If you have less than four characters, you can either go to the right side of the workflow and disable those detailer groups, OR you can just leave fields 1, 2, 3, and 6 empty in the "Char Identifier" node. For fields 4 and 5, put "0". 

If you disable the Character groups, make sure to set the "Set number of characters here" node to the correct number of characters so the Upscale module gets the right image to upscale.

"Universal Loras" apply to all body parts of all characters. The Upscaler Lora only applies to the Upscale portion. The "Upscaling Prompt" is optional. 

The "Denoise Levels" apply to all four characters simultaneously.

You can use pretty basic descriptions for your characters. The Prompt Enhancement handles adding language to describe things like natural cloth and skin details. 

The "Char Loras" section allows you to apply loras to specific body parts of specific characters.

Part of the point of this workflow is preventing you from having to go into these individual subgraphs, but there are some things that may force you to. 

1. You can only change the scheduler from here, there is a type mismatch that I don't know how to fix yet.

2. Changing the confidence levels for SAM3 or changing masking paramaters if the mask comes out messed up.

3. Looking at the mask previews. I may move that into its own block in a later version but the preview section is already pretty massive. 

4. Adjusting certain params on the SEGS detailer. 

5. Switching the upscale model.

The booleans on the "Prompt Enhancement" node are really important, so don't skip them. The subgraph contains logic that adds enhancements to your prompt based on these characteristics, so you either want to set them or bypass this subgraph to pass your prompting through unchanged.

For clothing, enable if the character is wearing any amount of clothing. 

"Pubic Hair" refers to neatly trimmed pubic hair, for women only. For males, you get what you get. :P

"Au Naturel" means full bush, also female only.

"Penetration" is specifically by a penis, of a vagina. For any other types of sexual penetration, you'll need to prompt manually. 

"Old" was prompted as 40+. "Not Old" is 18-25. For characters that are neither old nor young, default to "Old" and prompt for youthful features to counteract. 

For elderly characters, use "Old" and prompt for heavily aged features. 

If you decide to muck about in the prompt enhancer, look in Char 1 first for some documentation.

widgets_values: highly detailed male body, realistic masculine anatomy, varying thickness and texture of hair follicles, natural distribution of hair according to masculinity, the way hair interacts with the surface of the clothing, realistic fabric tension over masculine muscle structures, the shape and contour of the torso and limbs under the garment, subtle skin texture visible at the neckline or cuffs, authentic masculine physical presence, matte skin finish, unpolished biological realism, non-digital smoothness, no plastic or oily sheen, no airbrushed textures.

The prompt enhancement logic is split into separate sections for male and female characters. First, all of the booleans get passed into a series of "AND" and "XOR" gates to return a value of "True" for that particular characteristic and gender combination. So for example, an "AND" gate is used to do a Barefoot Female because "Female?" and "Barefoot?" are both true. An "XOR" gate is used for a female wearing shoes because "Female?" is true and "Barefoot?" is false.

Next, these values are hooked up to the "Switch String" node. You only need the "Something to String" node once per group. Again, male and female is split up, and these logic groups are further split up by the six refiner passes. You pass the string into string_a on the concatenate node, then to "on_false" on the next Switch String node. If the character state is true, the modified string is pass on. If false, the unmodified string is passed on.
The male and female logic nodes both always run, so these switches receive a correct prompt and an incorrect prompt, passing along the correct one based on the "Female?" flag.
